behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Real failures commonly occur at the module boundary between raw-body extraction, HMAC verification, and enqueue gating (e.g., verifier uses parsed JSON instead of raw bytes). Unit tests of the compare function miss that handoff bug. I rejected System because the behavior text only requires HTTP status + no forward, not real network/OS effects; in-process app wiring is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Run the webhook route in-process with real middleware stack and queue adapter test double. Send a request with a deliberately incorrect X-Signature-256 over a known raw body. Assert 401 and assert queue write was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across handler and persistent queue modules; the failure is a cross-module handoff/order bug, not a single-function bug. I rejected System because behavior text does not require real network/subprocess; ordering can be proven with in-process HTTP invocation plus a real local persistence-backed queue module.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process HTTP request to the handler with a real local persistent queue (or test DB). Instrument timestamps/hooks: queue commit completion and response send. Assert commit happens before response finalization.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is between request handler, enqueue, and async processor orchestration. A unit test of one function can miss accidental await/join across modules. I rejected System because no real OS/network primitive is required by the behavior text; a controlled in-process slow processor reproduces the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + processor in-process. Make processor intentionally block (e.g., deferred promise). Send valid event and assert response completes while processor is still pending; then release processor and confirm eventual processing.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time concurrency/SLA behavior. Mocked time or isolated module tests can hide event-loop starvation, socket/backpressure, and scheduling effects under concurrent load. I considered Integration but rejected it because the behavior explicitly requires "within 3 seconds wall-clock" under concurrency, which depends on real runtime behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real service process (or full runtime instance), pre-load slow concurrent downstream work, then send a new valid webhook over real HTTP and measure wall-clock latency. Assert HTTP response < 3s while prior slow jobs remain in progress.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Idempotency here is a state-handoff/persistence behavior (handler + dedupe keying + queue write path). Unit logic checks can miss duplicate insertion races or repository boundary mistakes. I rejected System because behavior text does not require real network/subprocess; local persistence/module wiring is enough.
behaviors.test_description: Using handler plus real dedupe/persistence layer, submit the same event ID twice. Assert first request writes once, second request does not create another queue record (and verify persisted count/key uniqueness).

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This checks translation of a queue-capacity signal across module boundary into HTTP semantics (status + header). Unit tests of mapping helpers are insufficient if wiring is wrong. I rejected System because no real OS/network behavior is required by the text; in-process route test with a full-queue condition is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Run route in-process with queue configured/forced to return "full". Send a valid webhook and assert HTTP 503 plus presence/value format of Retry-After header; assert no enqueue success side effect.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in queue module + persistence interaction (config, counting, write rejection). A pure unit test can miss storage-backed enforcement errors. I rejected System because the behavior text scopes to queue capacity enforcement and does not require real network/subprocess/OS-specific primitives beyond local persistence.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue against local test storage with capacity N. Enqueue N events successfully, then attempt N+1 and assert explicit rejection and unchanged persisted depth.
